item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image originates from here, provided by Ross Parry. As the website allows only non-commercial reuse of their content, the image is not compliant with CC 3.0 and has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know this falls outside of the 'few months' mentioned on the rules page, but this has been bothering me since I read this: why is a British ship responding to a distress call off the coast of Ireland?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While the writing is indeed good from a technical aspect, I kept on waiting for the shoe to drop at the end and for this to all make sense, but the explanation, after all that buildup, wasn't all that interesting. I dunno. Just isn't working for me.

That said, it's a good first article. Hope we get to see a second.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, I did a quick check myself and couldn't easily pull up any accurate info on the depths in that area either (though, from what I did find, they might tend closer to 100m) 30m is about 100 feet, and that's where it can start to kick in for some people. With trained divers for the Foundation, they'd probably be feeling it around 150, but be able to cope. But using different breathing mixes can prevent the effect, so that part is actually okay.

Diving solo is a no-go for recreational divers. Occupational divers, on the other hand, spend most of their time in the water alone. Not really sure what Search and Rescue protocols are on that regard, though.

+ Flashback to dive school
- Flashback to dive school

Occupational dive crew must have at least 3 people: Diver, Standby Diver, and Tender. Diver's role is to be in the water and do the work. Standby diver's responsibilities (only) include being ready to dive in an emergency, and maintaining communication with the diver. Tender does any other work required on topside, and assists the Standby Diver to get in the water in an emergency.

Two divers can dive at the same time, acting as standby to each other if communication with a diver on surface would be difficult or impossible, though they need a means of contact with each other in the water (I've heard 'visual contact' argued as adequate, though that's kind of a joke for an open water scenario)

For this line search operation a single diver, communicating with a tether line, would work like so:

  • After positioning the vessel and determining the search area (probably a 180º arc off the stern) the Diver gets in and swims to the end of their line, marking the far end of their search area.
  • The Standby Diver uses a predetermined set of line pulls to communicate with the Diver (i.e. 2 pulls = face the line and go to your right, 3 pulls = face the line and go your left, 4 pulls = come to surface, etc.) and guide them. When the Diver reaches the end of the arc, the Standby signals them to turn the other way.
  • The Diver works his way back towards the boat (number of passes required depends on visibility) until he finds his objective or is back at the boat.
  • If Diver finds it, he signals the Standby and they mark the co-ordinates. If not, determine a new search area and repeat the process.

I'll grant there are some industry regulations that Foundation would skirt around, but those would be for pragmatism and secrecy. If a couple-hundred feet of rope does the job to the same standard as a more expensive radio technology solution, the rope is going to be the preferred solution.

On the whole, if the general area the SCiP roams were a bit closer to shore it probably wouldn't hurt. But the procedures are actually reasonably explicable otherwise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ill take that in consideration when I get the chance! Not much time at the moment, but Ill give this article a wording and other minor adjustment edit, Ill also fixe the errors you mentionned.

You don't mention GPS readings in the CP. And isn't GPS an 'unmanned localization technique'? If that works, why not attach a locator to the ship, instead of sending divers to mark its location? Not clear! (OR, there's something I don't get about GPS!!)

Now this is a weak point indeed. I need to think on that, because I really don't want The Foundation to just slap a beacon on it and be done with it :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Regarding the nitrogen narcosis, I did not check the actual depth of the coordinate, can't access any oceanographic chart without paying for it. But I estimate from what I could research that the seabead is in the -30m, -50m range, would nitrogen narcosis be a serious and recurrent issue at this depth?

All of what you mentionned regarding the communications are good points, and Ill probably make minor changes on this aspect when I get the time to do that properly. Along with wording edits, the part your mentionned are coming up a lot in the comments about this skip, they do need to be changed.

For the diving team aspect, pretty much every diver I know told me that "diving alone is a no-go". I don't know if that is the golden standard, but I wrote it assuming that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I do understand the problem, Kmx.

A few nitpicks:

+ show block
– hide block

You write in the containment procedures:

◾A diving team must be sent to SCP-2346's last known coordinates and follow its expected path.

and in the description:

with unpredictable course variations

So, is it predictable or not?
Since apparently the ship leaves traces of its movements, you should probably write in the CP 'follow its tracks', or something like that.

GPS readings, performed as per containment procedure
Unmanned localization techniques, such as sonar imaging, were proven unable to detect SCP-2346.

You don't mention GPS readings in the CP. And isn't GPS an 'unmanned localization technique'? If that works, why not attach a locator to the ship, instead of sending divers to mark its location? Not clear! (OR, there's something I don't get about GPS!!)

His following report must be approached with extreme scrutiny.

Mmh. I don't think 'approached with scrutiny' is the right expression here… How about 'considered with caution' or something?

he and B. Howley
OF-2 Andrea Howley

So, is the initial of Howley's first name B or A? ;>

Okay, I think I know what's bothering me. Probably the lack of punchline - the end just peters out… Lacks some oompph. Could you perhaps think of something? It can just be a few words - a short sentence.

But I definitely like it. Here, no waiting, have my upvote! ;>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe the divers just got nitrogen narcosis from diving to the floor of the Atlantic 25km offshore?

Professional experience causes me to nitpick the diving SOPs a little..
Hide nitpicking

It seems like the diver's only contact with the surface is by wireless voice-comms. Thats good for reporting detailed information to topside, but for the safety of the diver they should use a physical tether line as well. It's more reliable for basic communication, and aids a standby diver in finding and recovering the original diver in case of emergency.
I can see maybe sending a pair down for experiments (so one can hang back while the other breaches the wreck), though even then they should probably use a buddy-line.
For the purposes of your Containment Procedures dives, they could be done by a single diver on just a tether-line. Because then he only needs to do a line-search and record the location of his target.
I'll stop myself here from lecturing further on commercial diving procedures, save that that this task might be done in a way that's simpler and safer.

In general, I rather like it. There are a few places where wording seems a bit off ('permanently assess communications' rather than constantly. 'exceptionally well-conserved' rather than 'well-preserved') though I'm not sure if it's wrong or just seems odd to me. So my vote is neutral for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do not recognize the bodies in the water.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All I could think was they couldn't recognize the bodies in the water. <.<

I dunno, this is atmospheric, but it's missing something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel the same way. I really like the idea of the carnivorous, mobile shipwreck. But as you say, something is not quite there, and I'm not sure what it is. It's a good idea and well-written, so I'm not sure what I think it's missing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for the correction.

Well, I can understand your frustration. But this being my first article, I did not want to go for something too far fetched, or requiering an exeptionnal execution to work. Ill keep going over it, and maybe there will be ways of adding more depth to it.

As for the name, you are right about the meaning. For the little story behind it, I intended to make SCP-2346 one of the French/Danish trawlers that were converted into light combat boat by the Kriegsmarine during WW2 and add some history bits about it, hence the German name. But I felt it was too "forced" and it did not feel right when working around that. But the name stayed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I corrected a typo: a thousand time -> a thousand times (in the second collapsible)

I like it but… I'm left a bit frustrated at the end. Problem is, I don't know why! There are other dangerous sunk ships on the site, and this one isn't particularly distinguishable from others. Needs more. But what? Shall be thinking about it! ;>
What is the meaning of the ship's name? Google translation says 'The Humble'. Is that it?

No vote for now. I'll get back to it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cold storage, or any closed room, yep.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I reworked the first part of the sentence to avoid a repetition.
Thank you for the fixes!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I guess cold storage is the ship's 'stomach?'

A few little typos, but otherwise +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I fixed a few terms that should've been hyphenated (visible in the page history), and

Local elevation profile allowed for minimal decompression stops between dives, letting Mason's team to perform multiple localization attempts.

Shouldn't that either be "letting Mason's team perform" or "allowing Mason's team to perform"? "Letting to" seems like a typo. I'd suggest the 'allowing' version because it seems more fitting tonally, but then again, you might not want two "allow"s in the same sentence. Maybe just rework that sentence in general?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thinked about that yes. Maybe the time limit should be in the containment procedure, and the recovery aspect kept in the description. I'll think about this a bit and edit in consequences.
Though the time limit is already in the containment procedure. I am not too sure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Any personnel exposed to SCP-2346 for more than 8 minutes must be removed from SCP-2346's vicinity and undergo medical examination. Recovering individuals will retain a high level of confusion and regularly express concerns about various tasks to be performed aboard SCP-2346. Over time, subjects will gradually lose their memory of SCP-2346 and will return to normal behaviour.

Wouldn't this paragraph go into the containment procedures?

Edit made after kmx37r's post: Ah okay, that makes enough sense.
